frontmatter.title: Graduate Paper Presentation & Best Practices Guide

children.value: Graduate students (ESS 512) are required to present
that demonstrates the application of computational methods learned in this course to real seismological research. This assignment develops critical skills in:
By completing this assignment, you will:
: Understand how methods from our computational labs are used in published research
: Assess the strengths and limitations of computational methods in real applications
: Evaluate whether the research could be reproduced from the information provided
: Present complex technical material clearly to peers
: Learn standards for publication-quality figures, code documentation, and method description
Your paper should connect to at least one of our computational modules:
Earthquake location algorithms, seismic tomography methods, phase identification
Regional dispersion analysis, ambient noise tomography, crustal thickness studies
Spectral analysis methods, attenuation (Q) measurement, time-frequency analysis
Network processing, data quality metrics, instrument response
Green’s function extraction, velocity monitoring, noise sources
Be published in a peer-reviewed journal (last 10 years preferred)
Bulletin of the Seismological Society of America (BSSA)
Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth (JGR)
: Submit 3 paper candidates to instructor via canvas
Brief (2-3 sentence) description of why each interests you
Which computational module(s) it connects to
: Instructor approves paper and assigns presentation week
: Send slides to instructor for feedback
(2 min): Scientific motivation and research question
(1 min): Main takeaway and connection to course
What seismic data was used? (Network, stations, events, time period)
3. Computational Methods (CORE - spend most time here)

children.label: 3. Computational Methods (CORE - spend most time here)

children.value: How does this connect to methods we learned in class?
Could you reproduce the results from the paper?
Which lectures relate to this work?
What extensions could we implement in class?
What did you learn that enhances your understanding of course material?
As part of your presentation, evaluate the paper against modern publication standards:
: Link to GitHub, Zenodo, or supplementary materials
: Specific versions of tools used (e.g., ObsPy 1.4.0)
Evaluate the main computational figure (choose one):
: If applicable, with label and units
: Include a screenshot showing good vs bad
: Synthetic tests or comparison with known results
Does the paper follow open science principles?
: Are datasets in public archives (IRIS, etc.)?
: How does this paper compare to ideal open science standards?
: PDF of slides to instructor for feedback
Title slide: Paper citation, your name, date
Final slide: 2-3 discussion questions for class
At least one slide showing code snippet or algorithm pseudocode
At least one slide with reproducibility checklist results
References slide (cite paper + any additional sources)
One limitation or improvement you would suggest
Clear description of algorithms; connection to course; technical accuracy
Thoughtful assessment of reproducibility, strengths, and limitations
Thorough evaluation of code/data availability, figures, documentation
Logical flow; concepts explained clearly; appropriate level for audience
All required elements included; accurate summary
Bensen et al. (2007). “Processing seismic ambient noise data to obtain reliable broad-band surface wave dispersion measurements.”
: Extremely detailed methods; became community standard
Lin et al. (2008). “Surface wave tomography of the western United States from ambient seismic noise: Rayleigh and Love wave phase velocity maps.”
: Clear workflow; connects noise to structure
VanDecar & Crosson (1990). “Determination of teleseismic relative phase arrival times using multi-channel cross-correlation and least squares.”
Rawlinson & Sambridge (2004). “Wave front evolution in strongly heterogeneous layered media using the fast marching method.”
Lawrence & Prieto (2011). “Attenuation tomography of the western United States from ambient seismic noise.”
Ringler et al. (2015). “Seismic station installation orientation errors at ANSS and IRIS/USGS stations.”
: Okay to screenshot (with citation) - don’t recreate
: Prepare for what classmates might ask
❌ Spending too much time on background/introduction
❌ Just summarizing results without explaining methods
✅ Clear explanation of algorithms (could we implement it?)
✅ Engaging visuals (flowcharts, diagrams, not just text)
Ten Simple Rules for Effective Presentation Slides:
(one student per week). Schedule determined after paper approval.
Contact instructor during office hours or via email. Start early - finding the right paper and understanding it deeply takes time!
